questions.prompt: A team of four students is building a study-app program over six weeks. Which collaboration practice is MOST likely to help the team produce a higher-quality program than any one student could alone?
choices: Each member writes their own version of the program separately, and the team picks the best one at the end.
The team holds regular meetings to share progress, divide tasks based on individual strengths, and review each other's code.
One member writes all the code while the others wait to test it once it is finished.
The team avoids using version control so that nobody's changes overwrite anyone else's work.
questions.correctText: The team holds regular meetings to share progress, divide tasks based on individual strengths, and review each other's code.
questions.explanation: Effective collaboration in the CSP development process relies on frequent communication, division of work that leverages diverse perspectives, and peer review. The other choices either isolate work, create a single point of failure, or avoid the tools (like version control) that make collaboration tractable.

questions.prompt: A developer is using an iterative design process. After getting user feedback on a prototype, what should the developer do NEXT?
choices: Ship the program to the public unchanged, because the prototype has already been built.
Delete the prototype and start over from scratch with a completely new design.
Revise the prototype based on the feedback, then test the new version with users again.
Wait until all features are added before considering any feedback.
questions.correctText: Revise the prototype based on the feedback, then test the new version with users again.
questions.explanation: Iterative development cycles through design, prototype, test, and revise. User feedback drives revisions, and the revised prototype is then re-tested. Starting over discards what works; shipping unchanged ignores the feedback entirely.

questions.prompt: A program is supposed to print the average of a list of numbers but instead prints the sum. What type of error is this?
choices: Syntax error — the code will not run at all.
Logic error — the code runs without crashing but produces an incorrect result.
Runtime error — the code crashes partway through execution.
Overflow error — the result is too large to store.
questions.correctText: Logic error — the code runs without crashing but produces an incorrect result.
questions.explanation: A logic error means the code is syntactically valid and executes successfully, but the algorithm produces the wrong output (sum instead of average). Syntax errors prevent execution; runtime errors crash mid-run; overflow is a specific numeric runtime issue.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the MOST effective debugging strategy when a program produces unexpected output?
choices: Delete the program and rewrite it from memory.
Insert DISPLAY statements at key points to inspect variable values as the program runs.
Run the program many times hoping the bug fixes itself.
Add more features so the bug is less noticeable.
questions.correctText: Insert DISPLAY statements at key points to inspect variable values as the program runs.
questions.explanation: Inserting print/DISPLAY statements (or using a debugger) lets you trace how variables change at each step so you can isolate where the program's behavior diverges from your expectation. The other strategies waste effort or mask the underlying problem.

questions.prompt: Why is program documentation (such as comments and a README) considered a critical part of the development process?
choices: It is required by all programming languages or the code will not compile.
It makes the program run faster by giving the computer extra instructions.
It helps the developer and collaborators understand, maintain, and modify the program later.
It is the only way to protect a program from being copied.
questions.correctText: It helps the developer and collaborators understand, maintain, and modify the program later.
questions.explanation: Documentation captures intent, design decisions, and usage information so future readers (including the original author months later) can understand and safely modify the code. Comments are ignored by the interpreter and have no effect on performance or copy protection.

questions.prompt: A student writes the line `IF (score > 100` and the program refuses to run, reporting an error before any output appears. This is BEST classified as which kind of error?
choices: Logic error
Syntax error
Runtime error
Overflow error
questions.correctText: Syntax error
questions.explanation: The missing closing parenthesis violates the grammar of the language, so the interpreter or compiler rejects the program before execution begins. That is the definition of a syntax error. Logic errors only appear when running code produces wrong results.

questions.prompt: A program stores unsigned integers using exactly 4 bits. What happens when the program tries to compute 15 + 1?
choices: The result is 16, stored normally with no issues.
The result overflows because 16 cannot be represented in 4 bits, and the stored value will be incorrect.
The program automatically switches to 8-bit storage and stores 16.
The program rounds the result down to the nearest power of 2.
questions.correctText: The result overflows because 16 cannot be represented in 4 bits, and the stored value will be incorrect.
questions.explanation: With 4 bits you can represent 2^4 = 16 distinct unsigned values (0 through 15). The value 16 requires a 5th bit; storing it in 4 bits causes overflow and the result wraps or is otherwise incorrect. Languages do not silently widen storage on the AP CSP exam model.

questions.prompt: ASCII uses 7 bits per character, while a common Unicode encoding (UTF-8) uses 1 to 4 bytes per character. Which statement BEST explains why Unicode was created?
choices: Unicode runs faster than ASCII on modern processors.
ASCII can only represent 128 distinct characters, which is too few to cover the world's writing systems, symbols, and emoji.
Unicode is the only encoding that supports the English alphabet.
ASCII characters cannot be transmitted over the internet.
questions.correctText: ASCII can only represent 128 distinct characters, which is too few to cover the world's writing systems, symbols, and emoji.
questions.explanation: 7-bit ASCII gives 2^7 = 128 codepoints, enough for English letters, digits, and basic punctuation but not for Chinese, Arabic, Devanagari, mathematical symbols, or emoji. Unicode was designed with a much larger codespace to encode characters across all human writing systems.

questions.prompt: A typical RGB color in a web image uses 24 bits — 8 bits per red, green, and blue channel. How many distinct colors can be represented?
choices: approximately 16.8 million
approximately 4.3 billion
questions.correctText: approximately 16.8 million
questions.explanation: Each channel has 2^8 = 256 possible intensities, so the total number of color combinations is 256 × 256 × 256 = 2^24 ≈ 16,777,216 — roughly 16.8 million.

questions.prompt: Which file-format pairing correctly matches the compression type used?
choices: PNG = lossy; JPEG = lossless; MP3 = lossless
PNG = lossless; JPEG = lossy; MP3 = lossy
PNG = lossy; JPEG = lossy; MP3 = lossless
PNG = lossless; JPEG = lossless; MP3 = lossless
questions.correctText: PNG = lossless; JPEG = lossy; MP3 = lossy
questions.explanation: PNG uses lossless compression so the original pixel data can be reconstructed exactly. JPEG discards perceptually less important image detail (lossy). MP3 discards inaudible audio frequencies and masking artifacts (lossy). Lossy formats sacrifice fidelity for smaller files.

questions.prompt: A photo file taken with a phone contains pixel data and ALSO information such as the date taken, GPS coordinates, camera model, and shutter speed. What is the term for this extra information?
choices: Source code
Metadata
Checksum
Ciphertext
questions.correctText: Metadata
questions.explanation: Metadata is 'data about data' — information that describes the primary data. For a photo, the pixels are the data and EXIF fields (date, GPS, camera settings) are metadata. Removing metadata before sharing a photo is a common privacy step.

questions.prompt: A weather app displays a single temperature reading to the user instead of all the raw atmospheric sensor readings that produced it. This is an example of which key concept?
choices: Data abstraction
Lossy compression
Encryption
Crowdsourcing
questions.correctText: Data abstraction
questions.explanation: Data abstraction hides lower-level details and exposes only what the user needs. The complex sensor measurements are summarized into a single, useful number, letting the user reason about temperature without managing the raw data.

questions.prompt: Which visualization is the BEST choice for showing the relationship between two numerical variables (for example, hours studied vs. test score)?
choices: Pie chart
Bar chart
Scatter plot
Single-value gauge
questions.correctText: Scatter plot
questions.explanation: A scatter plot places each observation as a point in two-dimensional space, making correlations, clusters, and outliers between two numerical variables visible. Pie charts show parts of a whole; bar charts compare categories; gauges show one value.

questions.prompt: Which list correctly names the three fundamental building blocks of algorithms identified in the AP CSP curriculum?
choices: Sequencing, selection, and iteration
Variables, functions, and classes
Input, processing, and output
Compilation, interpretation, and execution
questions.correctText: Sequencing, selection, and iteration
questions.explanation: Any algorithm can be built from sequencing (run steps in order), selection (choose between alternatives with IF/IF-ELSE), and iteration (repeat steps with loops). The other choices describe program parts or execution stages, not algorithmic structure.

questions.prompt: Consider the Boolean expression: (age >= 16) AND (hasPermit = true). For which case does the expression evaluate to true?
choices: age is 15 and hasPermit is true
age is 17 and hasPermit is false
age is 16 and hasPermit is true
age is 14 and hasPermit is false
questions.correctText: age is 16 and hasPermit is true
questions.explanation: AND requires both sides to be true. Only the choice with age >= 16 AND hasPermit = true satisfies both conditions. The first fails the age test; the second fails the permit test; the fourth fails both.

choices: hot
warm
cool
Nothing is displayed.
questions.correctText: warm
questions.explanation: 75 is not greater than 90, so the outer ELSE branch runs. Inside it, 75 is greater than 70, so the nested IF prints 'warm'.

questions.prompt: Which of the following BEST describes the role of parameters in a procedure?
choices: They are global variables that any procedure can read or change.
They are placeholders that receive specific input values each time the procedure is called, allowing the procedure to be reused with different inputs.
They are the return values that the procedure sends back to the caller.
They are comments inside the procedure that explain what it does.
questions.correctText: They are placeholders that receive specific input values each time the procedure is called, allowing the procedure to be reused with different inputs.
questions.explanation: Parameters generalize a procedure. By passing different argument values to the same parameter, you can reuse one procedure for many inputs without rewriting it. Return values are sent back, comments document code, and globals are a separate concept.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of an undecidable problem?
choices: Sorting a list of one million integers into ascending order.
Determining for an arbitrary program and input whether the program will eventually halt or run forever.
Computing the average of a list of test scores.
Finding the largest value in a finite list.
questions.correctText: Determining for an arbitrary program and input whether the program will eventually halt or run forever.
questions.explanation: The Halting Problem is the canonical undecidable problem — no algorithm can correctly decide for every program/input pair whether execution will terminate. The other tasks are routine and decidable in finite time.

questions.prompt: A sorted list contains 1,000,000 items. Which statement BEST compares linear search and binary search on this list?
choices: Linear search is always faster because it checks items in order.
Binary search is generally much faster because it eliminates roughly half of the remaining items at each step, while linear search may check up to all items.
Both algorithms take exactly the same time on any input.
Binary search only works on unsorted lists.
questions.correctText: Binary search is generally much faster because it eliminates roughly half of the remaining items at each step, while linear search may check up to all items.
questions.explanation: Binary search runs in O(log n) on a sorted list (about 20 steps for a million items), while linear search runs in O(n) (up to a million steps). Binary search requires the list to be sorted; linear search has no such requirement.

questions.prompt: A meteorologist uses a computer program to model how a hurricane might move based on temperature, pressure, and wind data. The program is BEST described as which of the following?
choices: A simulation that abstracts a real-world phenomenon to make predictions
A binary search of historical hurricane data
A lossless compression algorithm
A public-key encryption system
questions.correctText: A simulation that abstracts a real-world phenomenon to make predictions
questions.explanation: Simulations build simplified, abstract models of real-world systems to explore scenarios that would be too costly, slow, or dangerous to test directly. Hurricane forecasting is a classic example.

choices: The exact number of sides on a die
An approximation of how many times a fair six-sided die rolls a 6 in 1000 rolls (about 167)
The value 1000 every time the program is run
The largest value produced by the random function
questions.correctText: An approximation of how many times a fair six-sided die rolls a 6 in 1000 rolls (about 167)
questions.explanation: The loop simulates 1000 die rolls; each roll has a 1-in-6 chance of equaling 6. Over 1000 trials, count will hover around 1000/6 ≈ 167, varying slightly each run because of randomness. This is a Monte Carlo simulation.

questions.prompt: When a large file is sent over the Internet, it is broken into smaller units that are sent independently, possibly along different routes, and reassembled at the destination. What are these units called?
choices: Packets
Pixels
Cookies
Cores
questions.correctText: Packets
questions.explanation: The Internet uses packet switching: data is split into packets that travel independently across routers and are reassembled at the destination. This makes the network resilient and efficient even when individual links fail or congest.

questions.prompt: What is the primary role of the Domain Name System (DNS)?
choices: Encrypt all web traffic between browsers and servers.
Translate human-readable domain names (like example.org) into the numeric IP addresses computers use to route messages.
Compress images before they are sent over the network.
Store every user's login passwords in a central database.
questions.correctText: Translate human-readable domain names (like example.org) into the numeric IP addresses computers use to route messages.
questions.explanation: DNS is the Internet's distributed directory: it maps memorable names to IP addresses so browsers and other clients can connect to the right server. Encryption is HTTPS/TLS; compression is a separate concern; DNS does not store passwords.

questions.prompt: Which statement BEST describes the relationship between TCP and IP in the Internet protocol stack?
choices: TCP encrypts data; IP compresses it.
IP is responsible for routing packets between hosts using addresses, and TCP is responsible for reliable, ordered delivery of those packets on top of IP.
TCP and IP are competing standards; only one is used at a time on a given network.
TCP assigns domain names; IP serves web pages.
questions.correctText: IP is responsible for routing packets between hosts using addresses, and TCP is responsible for reliable, ordered delivery of those packets on top of IP.
questions.explanation: IP (Internet Protocol) handles addressing and routing of individual packets. TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) sits on top and adds reliability — acknowledging packets, retransmitting lost ones, and delivering bytes in order to the application.

questions.prompt: When a user visits a site whose URL begins with `https://` instead of `http://`, what is the MOST important difference?
choices: HTTPS pages always load faster than HTTP pages.
HTTPS encrypts the data exchanged between the browser and the server so that intermediaries cannot read or tamper with it.
HTTPS pages are stored on the user's device permanently and HTTP pages are not.
HTTPS does not require an Internet connection.
questions.correctText: HTTPS encrypts the data exchanged between the browser and the server so that intermediaries cannot read or tamper with it.
questions.explanation: HTTPS = HTTP over TLS. The transport is encrypted, which protects confidentiality and integrity of the request and response. HTTPS may be slightly slower than HTTP because of the handshake, and both require an Internet connection.

questions.prompt: Which pairing correctly defines latency and bandwidth?
choices: Latency = the amount of data that can be sent per unit time; Bandwidth = the time it takes for a message to travel from sender to receiver.
Latency = the time it takes for a message to travel from sender to receiver; Bandwidth = the amount of data that can be sent per unit time.
Latency and bandwidth are different names for the same quantity.
Latency = the cost of an Internet plan; Bandwidth = the number of devices on a network.
questions.correctText: Latency = the time it takes for a message to travel from sender to receiver; Bandwidth = the amount of data that can be sent per unit time.
questions.explanation: Latency is delay (often measured in milliseconds). Bandwidth is throughput (often measured in bits per second). A connection can have high bandwidth but high latency, or low latency but low bandwidth — the two are independent.

questions.prompt: Which feature of the Internet's design BEST supports fault tolerance?
choices: There is exactly one cable connecting every pair of cities.
Every packet must travel along the same fixed route from sender to receiver.
Redundant connections among routers allow packets to take alternate paths when a link or router fails.
All routing decisions are made by a single central server.
questions.correctText: Redundant connections among routers allow packets to take alternate paths when a link or router fails.
questions.explanation: Fault tolerance comes from redundancy. Because there are many possible paths between any two endpoints, the failure of one router or link does not bring the network down — packets simply route around the failure.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the BEST description of cloud computing?
choices: Storing data and running programs on remote servers accessed over the Internet rather than on a user's local device.
A new type of weather-prediction software.
A wireless protocol that replaces Wi-Fi in homes.
An algorithm for compressing video files.
questions.correctText: Storing data and running programs on remote servers accessed over the Internet rather than on a user's local device.
questions.explanation: Cloud computing delivers computing resources (storage, processing, software) as services hosted in remote data centers and accessed over the network. Users gain scalability and accessibility but depend on connectivity and provider trust.

questions.prompt: Which scenario BEST illustrates a peer-to-peer (P2P) model rather than a client-server model?
choices: A web browser requests a page from a central web server, which sends back the HTML.
A mail client downloads new messages from a mail server.
Users share files directly with each other through a file-sharing network, with each user's computer acting as both supplier and consumer.
A student streams a video from a video-hosting company's server.
questions.correctText: Users share files directly with each other through a file-sharing network, with each user's computer acting as both supplier and consumer.
questions.explanation: In P2P, every participant is both client and server, exchanging data directly with peers. Client-server architectures designate one party as the service provider and the other as the requester. Web, mail, and streaming are typical client-server.

questions.prompt: The phrase 'digital divide' refers to which of the following?
choices: The split between operating systems made by different companies.
Unequal access to computing devices, reliable Internet, and digital literacy across different communities and regions.
The separation of digital files into folders on a hard drive.
The legal boundary between online and offline behavior.
questions.correctText: Unequal access to computing devices, reliable Internet, and digital literacy across different communities and regions.
questions.explanation: The digital divide describes disparities in access to and ability to use computing technology — disparities that fall along income, geographic, age, and other lines and that can reinforce broader inequalities.

questions.prompt: An astronomy project invites volunteers around the world to use a website to classify galaxy shapes in telescope images, helping researchers analyze data sets too large for any one team. This is the BEST example of which concept?
choices: Citizen science / crowdsourcing
Symmetric encryption
A denial-of-service attack
Lossy compression
questions.correctText: Citizen science / crowdsourcing
questions.explanation: Crowdsourcing distributes work to a large group of contributors over the Internet; when the work is scientific research, it is often called citizen science. The other terms describe cryptography, attacks, or file compression.

questions.prompt: A photographer wants others to be able to copy and remix her photos as long as they give her credit and do not use them commercially. Which approach BEST fits her goal?
choices: Publish the photos with no notice at all, since unmarked work is automatically free for all uses.
Apply an appropriate Creative Commons license (such as CC BY-NC) that specifies attribution and a non-commercial restriction.
Encrypt the photos so nobody can ever view them.
Place the photos in the public domain so all rights, including attribution, are waived.
questions.correctText: Apply an appropriate Creative Commons license (such as CC BY-NC) that specifies attribution and a non-commercial restriction.
questions.explanation: Creative Commons licenses let creators grant specific permissions while retaining copyright. A BY-NC license requires attribution and forbids commercial use, matching the goal. Unmarked work is still under copyright; public domain waives attribution; encryption prevents viewing.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the BEST example of Personally Identifiable Information (PII)?
choices: The temperature in a city yesterday
A social security number combined with a full legal name
The total number of users of a website
Today's stock market closing index
questions.correctText: A social security number combined with a full legal name
questions.explanation: PII is information that can be used, alone or in combination, to identify a specific individual. A name plus SSN is a classic high-risk combination. Aggregate or public statistics about places or markets are not PII.

questions.prompt: Which statement BEST distinguishes symmetric encryption from public-key (asymmetric) encryption?
choices: Symmetric encryption uses the same key to encrypt and decrypt, while public-key encryption uses a public key to encrypt and a different, private key to decrypt.
Symmetric encryption is unbreakable, while public-key encryption is easily broken.
Public-key encryption requires no keys at all.
Symmetric encryption only works for images, and public-key encryption only works for text.
questions.correctText: Symmetric encryption uses the same key to encrypt and decrypt, while public-key encryption uses a public key to encrypt and a different, private key to decrypt.
questions.explanation: Symmetric encryption requires both parties to share one secret key, which complicates key distribution. Public-key (asymmetric) systems use a mathematically linked key pair so anyone can encrypt with the public key, but only the holder of the private key can decrypt.

questions.prompt: A school requires students to enter their password AND a one-time code sent to their phone before logging in to grades. This is BEST described as which security practice?
choices: Multifactor authentication
Symmetric encryption
A phishing attack
A denial-of-service defense
questions.correctText: Multifactor authentication
questions.explanation: Multifactor authentication (MFA) combines two or more independent factors — typically something you know (password), something you have (phone for the one-time code), or something you are (biometric). Even if one factor is stolen, the account is still protected.
